front: What is the primary equation that defines the probability density function (PDF) of a normal distribution?
back: f(x | μ, σ²) = (1 / √(2πσ²)) * e^(-(x-μ)²/(2σ²)), where μ is the mean and σ² is the variance.

front: What is the '68-95-99.7' rule, and which distribution does it describe?
back: It is the empirical rule for a normal distribution. It states that approximately 68%, 95%, and 99.7% of the data fall within one, two, and three standard deviations (σ) of the mean (μ), respectively.

front: Why is the normal distribution critically important in statistical machine learning?
back: It is a foundational assumption for many models and methods (e.g., linear regression, Gaussian Naive Bayes). Its properties are mathematically convenient, and the Central Limit Theorem justifies its use for the distribution of sample means.

front: What is a standard normal distribution?
back: A special case of the normal distribution with a mean (μ) of 0 and a standard deviation (σ) of 1. Any normal distribution can be transformed into a standard normal using z-score standardization: z = (x - μ) / σ.

front: How is the normal distribution parameterized, and what do these parameters control?
back: It is parameterized by two values: mean (μ) and variance (σ²). The mean μ determines the center/location of the distribution, and the variance σ² controls the spread or width (dispersion) of the curve.
